subject: Mathematics
q: If a complex number $z$ statisfies the equation $x+\sqrt{2}|z+1|+i=0$, then $|z|$ is equal to :
question: If a complex number $z$ statisfies the equation $x+\sqrt{2}|z+1|+i=0$, then $|z|$ is equal to :
solution:
Given equation is
$$
\begin{aligned}
& z+\sqrt{2}|z+1|+i=0 \\
& \text { put } z=x+i y \text { in the given equation. } \\
& (x+i y)+\sqrt{2}|x+i y+1|+i=0 \\
& \Rightarrow x+i y+\sqrt{2}\left[\sqrt{(x+1)^2+y^2}\right]+i=0
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, equating real and imaginary part, we get
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+\sqrt{2} \sqrt{(x+1)^2+y^2}=0 \text { and } \\
& y+1=0 \Rightarrow y=-1 \\
& \Rightarrow x+\sqrt{2} \sqrt{(x+1)^2+(-1)^2}=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \sqrt{2} \sqrt{(x+1)^2+1}=-x \\
& \Rightarrow 2\left[(x+1)^2+1\right]=x^2 \\
& \Rightarrow x^2+4 x+4=0 \\
& \Rightarrow x=-2
\end{aligned}
$$
Thus, $z=-2+i(-1) \Rightarrow|z|=\sqrt{5}$

solution:
Given that the setRez-z¯+zz¯2-3z+5z¯:z∈ℂ, Rez=3 is equal to the interval (α,β].

Let z=x+iy

=Rex+iy-x-iy+x2+y22-3x+iy+5x-iy

=Rex2+y2+i2y2+2x-8iy

=Rex2+y2+2yi21+x+8iy21+x2+8y2

=2x2+y21+x-16y241+x2+8y2

Now using, Rez=3⇒x=3 we get,

=89+y2-16y264+64y2

⇒fy=189-y21+y2

Let t=189-y21+y2

⇒8t+8ty2=9-y2

⇒y28t+1+8t-9=0

Now D≥0

⇒02-48t+18t-9≥0

⇒t--18t-98≤0

⇒t∈(-18,98] as t≠-18

Range of fy=(-0.125,1.125]

⇒α=- 0.125

⇒β=1.125 

⇒β-α=1.25

⇒24β-α=30

Hence this is the required solution.

question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>γ</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>δ</mi></math> are the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>4</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2021</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>2021</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>γ</mi><mn>2021</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>δ</mi><mn>2021</mn></msup></math> is equal to
solution:
We know that x4+x3+x2+x+1=0 can be written as 

x5-1x-1=0⇒  x5=1

i.e. x=115

So α, β, γ and δ are the 5th  roots of unity except 1.

Now, from the concept of roots of unity, we know

 αn+βn+γn+δn+1n=0if n is not a multiple of 55if n is a multiple of 5

So, α2021+β2021+γ2021+δ2021=-1.

∵z2=zz¯ & α¯z+αz¯¯=α¯z¯+αz¯¯=αz¯+α¯z

Then, azz¯+αz¯+α¯z+d=0 or zz¯+αaz¯+α¯az+da=0 is the equation of circle. 

Centre =-αa, radius, r=αα¯a2-da=αα¯-ada2

αa2-da≥0 for the equation to represent circle.

So, |α|2-ad>0 and a∈R-{0}

If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>≠</mo><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is a point on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> such that the line through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> is perpendicular to the line through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>arg</mi><mfenced><mi>z</mi></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
z1≡A≡3,4, z2≡B≡4,3, z3≡C≡0,5

and z≡P≡x,y

Slope of BC=3-54-0=-12

Slope of AP=2

Now, equation of AP is y-4=2x-3

⇒y=2x-1

Since P lies on circle x2+y2=25

⇒x2+2x-12=25

⇒x=-75 and y=-245

⇒arg(z)=tan-1247-π

subject: Mathematics
q: If $z \neq 1$ and $\frac{z^2}{z-1}$ is real, then the point represented by the complex number $z$ lies
question: If $z \neq 1$ and $\frac{z^2}{z-1}$ is real, then the point represented by the complex number $z$ lies
options: either on the real axis or on a circle passing through the origin
on a circle with centre at the origin
either on the real axis or on a circle not passing through the origin
solution:
Let $z=x+$ iy $(\because x \neq 1$ as $z \neq 1)$
$z^2=\left(x^2-y^2\right)+i(2 x y)$
$\frac{z^2}{z-1}$ is real 
$\Rightarrow$ its imaginary part $=0$
$\Rightarrow 2 x y(x-1)-y\left(x^2-y^2\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow y\left(x^2+y^2-2 x\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow y=0 ; x^2+y^2-2 x=0$
$\therefore \mathrm{z}$ lies either on real axis or on a circle through origin.

options: Is strictly greater than <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math> but less than <math> <mrow> <mfrac> <mrow> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> </mrow> </mfrac> </mrow> </math>
solution:
z≥2  i.e.,  z-0≥2

∴  The distance of 'z' from origin is greater than two.
We have to find minimum of z+12

z+12=z--12 = distance of z from -12, 0.

On the argand plane,



z lies in the dotted regions I, II, III, IV.

∴    The minimum distance is from the point B=2-12 = 3 2

⇒32∈1, 2

q:
For two non-zero complex number z1 and z2, if Rez1z2=0 and Rez1+z2=0, then which of the following are possible?

(A) Imz1>0 and Imz2>0
(B) Imz1<0 and Imz2>0
(C) Imz1>0 and Imz2<0
(D) Imz1<0 and Imz2<0

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
For two non-zero complex number z1 and z2, if Rez1z2=0 and Rez1+z2=0, then which of the following are possible?

(A) Imz1>0 and Imz2>0
(B) Imz1<0 and Imz2>0
(C) Imz1>0 and Imz2<0
(D) Imz1<0 and Imz2<0

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
Let,

z1=x1+iy1 and z2=x2+iy2

∵Rez1z2=0

x1x2-y1y2=0......i

∵Rez1+z2=0

x1+x2=0......ii

From equations i and ii we get

x12+y1y2=0

⇒y1y2=-x12

Therefore Im(z1) and Im(z2) are of opposite sign

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>i</mi><mi>y</mi></math> satisfies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mi>z</mi></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>i</mi><mi>y</mi></math> satisfies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mi>z</mi></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then
solution:
Given, z=x+iy

Also, z=2

So, x2+y2=4     ...1

Now, z-i=z+5i

⇒z-i2=z+5i2

⇒x2+y-12=x2+y+52

⇒y=-2     ...2

So, x=0 by solving equation 1 & 2

Hence, only x+2y+4=0 is true, as point only satisfy this equation.

subject: Mathematics
q: A complex number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> is said to be unimodular if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mi>z</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are complex numbers such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><menclose notation="top"><mi>z</mi></menclose><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> is unimodular and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is not unimodular, then the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> lies on a
question: A complex number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi></math> is said to be unimodular if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mi>z</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are complex numbers such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><menclose notation="top"><mi>z</mi></menclose><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> is unimodular and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is not unimodular, then the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> lies on a
solution:
Given, z1-2z22-z1z¯2  is unimodular. 

⇒ z1-2z22-z1z¯2=1

⇒ z1-2z2=2-z1z¯2

Squaring both the sides, we get,

z1-2z22=2-z1z22

⇒ z1-2z2z¯1-2z¯2=2-z1z¯22-z¯1z2

∵ z2=zz¯

⇒  z1z¯1−2z1z¯2−2z¯1z2+4z¯2z¯2

=4-2z¯1 z2-2z1 z¯2+z1 z¯1 z2 z¯2

⇒ z12+4z22=4+z12 z22

⇒ z12-4+4z22-z12 z22=0

⇒ z12-41-z22=0

⇒ z1=2 or z2=1

 Given, z2 is not unimodular

∴  z1=2

∴   Point z1 lies on a circle of radius 2.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>≤</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>z</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>z</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>≤</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> attains minimum and maximum values, respectively, at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>∈</mo><mi>S</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>∈</mo><mi>S</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mfenced><mrow><msup><mfenced close="|" open="|"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced close="|" open="|"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are integers, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></math> is equal to ______.
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>:</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>≤</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>z</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mover><mi>z</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>≤</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> attains minimum and maximum values, respectively, at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>∈</mo><mi>S</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>∈</mo><mi>S</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mfenced><mrow><msup><mfenced close="|" open="|"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mfenced close="|" open="|"><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are integers, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></math> is equal to ______.
solution:
Given z-2≤1

x-22+y2≤1     ⋯i represents interior region of a circle with centre 2,0 and radius 1

Now z1+i+z¯1-i≤2

Puting z=x+iy, we get

x+iy1+i+x-iy1-i≤2

⇒x-y+ix+y+x-y-ix+y≤2

∴ x-y≤1    …i

Let A1,0 and B2,1 be the points on the line x-y=1 and the circle x-22+y2=1

z-4i represents the distance from P0,4 to any point in the region S

Here PA=17, PB=13 

So A1,0 is the point representing z2

Let D2+cosθ,0+sinθ be the point representing z1

∴ mCP=tanθ=-2

cosθ=-15,sinθ=25

∴  D≡2-15,25

⇒z1=2-15+2i5

So z1=25-455 & z2=1

∴ z22=1

∴ 5z12+z22=30-45

∴α=30

β=-4

Hence α+β=26

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\left|z^2-1\right|=|z|^2+1$, then $z$ lies on
question: If $\left|z^2-1\right|=|z|^2+1$, then $z$ lies on
solution:
$\left|z^2-1\right|^2=\left(|z|^2+1\right) \Rightarrow\left(z^2-1\right)\left(\bar{z}^2-1\right)=|z|^4+2|z|^2+1$
$\Rightarrow z^2+\bar{z}^2+2 z \bar{z}=0 \Rightarrow z+\bar{z}=0$
$\Rightarrow R(z)=0 \Rightarrow z$ lies on the imaginary axis.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math><mi>z</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>C</mi></math> be such that <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><mi>ω</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>z</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then:
question: Let <math><mi>z</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>C</mi></math> be such that <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo><</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><mi>ω</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>5</mn><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>z</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then:
solution:
Given z<1 and ω=5+3z51-z

⇒5ω1-z=5+3z

⇒5ω-5ωz=5+3z

⇒z=5ω-53+5ω

Using the given condition z=5ω-13+5ω<1


⇒5ω-1<3+5ω

⇒5ω-1<5ω+35

⇒ω-1<ω--35

We know that the locus of a complex number z1 satisfying z1-a=z2-b is the perpendicular bisector of the line segment joining the points a and b.

Hence, the locus of the point ω satisfying ω-1=ω--35 is the line x=1-352=15

Hence, Reω>15

⇒5Reω>1.

q:
Let ω be a complex number such that 2ω+1=z where z=-3 . If

1111-ω2-1ω21ω2ω7=3k,

Then k can be equal to:
question:
Let ω be a complex number such that 2ω+1=z where z=-3 . If

1111-ω2-1ω21ω2ω7=3k,

Then k can be equal to:
solution:
ω= -12+32 i (cube root of unity)

So, 1+ω+ω2=0  and  ω3=1

Now 1111ωω21ω2ω=3ω2-ω

So k=ω2-ω

⇒k=-12-32i--12+32i

⇒k=-3i

⇒k=-z

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $O$ be the origin, the point $A$ be $z_1=\sqrt{3}+2 \sqrt{2} i$, the point $B\left(z_2\right)$ be such that $\sqrt{3}\left|z_2\right|=\left|z_1\right|$ and $\arg \left(z_2\right)=\arg \left(z_1\right)+\frac{\pi}{6}$. Then
question: Let $O$ be the origin, the point $A$ be $z_1=\sqrt{3}+2 \sqrt{2} i$, the point $B\left(z_2\right)$ be such that $\sqrt{3}\left|z_2\right|=\left|z_1\right|$ and $\arg \left(z_2\right)=\arg \left(z_1\right)+\frac{\pi}{6}$. Then
options: ABO is an obtuse angled isosceles triangle
solution: $\begin{aligned} & O A=\left|z_1\right|=\sqrt{3+8}=\sqrt{11} \\ & \text { and } O B=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\left|z_1\right|=\sqrt{\frac{11}{3}} \\ & A B^2=O A^2+O B^2-2 \cdot O A \cdot O B \cos \frac{\pi}{6} \\ & \quad=11+\frac{11}{3}-2 \cdot \frac{11}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad A B=\sqrt{\frac{11}{3}} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Area of } \triangle A B D=\frac{1}{2} \cdot O A \cdot O B \cdot \sin \frac{\pi}{6} \\ & \quad=\frac{11}{4 \sqrt{3}} \text { sq. units } \end{aligned}$ Here $O B=A B$ and $\angle A=\frac{2 \pi}{3}$ $\therefore \quad \triangle A B D$ is an obtuse angled isosceles triangle.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the real part of the complex number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>i</mi><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of the integral <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mn>0</mn><mi>θ</mi></msubsup><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to:
question: If the real part of the complex number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>i</mi><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mo>(</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>π</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of the integral <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mn>0</mn><mi>θ</mi></msubsup><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
Let

z=11-cosθ+2isinθ

⇒z=1-cosθ-2isinθ1-cosθ+2isinθ1-cosθ-2isinθ

⇒z=2sin2θ2-2isinθ(1-cosθ)2+4sin2θ

Put,sinθ=2sinθ2cosθ2, 1-cosθ=2sin2θ2

⇒z=2sin2θ2-4isinθ2cosθ24sin4θ2+16sin2θ2cos2θ2

Hence,

Rez=2sin2θ24sin4θ2+16sin2θ2cos2θ2

⇒12sin2θ2+8cos2θ2=15

⇒12sin2θ2+8cos2θ2=15

⇒sin2θ2+4cos2θ2=52

⇒1-cos2θ2+4cos2θ2=52

⇒3cos2θ2=32

⇒cos2θ2=12

⇒θ2=nπ±π4, n∈Z

⇒θ=2nπ±π2

For θ∈(0,π), θ=π2.

Hence,

∴∫0θsinθdθ=∫0π2sinθdθ=-cosθ0π2

=-0-1

=1

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>:</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>i</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>C</mi><mo>:</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>i</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is a straight line in the complex plane
solution:
Given z-iz+2i∈R

Then argz-iz+2i is 0 or π

⇒S is straight line in complex plane.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $S=\{z: 3 \leqslant|2 z-3(1+i)| \leqslant 7\}$ be a set of complex numbers. Then $\min _{z \in S}\left|\left(z+\frac{1}{2}(5+3 i)\right)\right|$ is equal to :
question: Let $S=\{z: 3 \leqslant|2 z-3(1+i)| \leqslant 7\}$ be a set of complex numbers. Then $\min _{z \in S}\left|\left(z+\frac{1}{2}(5+3 i)\right)\right|$ is equal to :
solution:
$\dfrac{3}{2} \leq \left| z - \dfrac{3}{2}(1+i) \right| \leq \dfrac{7}{2}$

$\min_{z \in S} \left| z - \left(\dfrac{-5}{2} - \dfrac{3}{2}i\right) \right| = PB$
$PB = PC - \dfrac{7}{2} \Rightarrow 5 - \dfrac{7}{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{3}{2}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></math> be a complex number such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>z</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math>, then the maximum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mi>z</mi></mfenced></math> is
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi><mo>≠</mo><mn>0</mn></math> be a complex number such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>z</mi></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math>, then the maximum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mi>z</mi></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Given z-1z=2

We know that z-1z≤z-1z≤z+1z Let z=r

i.e. r-1r≤2 and 

r+1r≥2 which is always true.

Now r-1r≤2

⇒r-1r≥-2 & r-1r≤2

⇒r2-1≤2r

⇒r-12≤2

⇒r-1≤2

∴ zmax=1+2

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math><msub><mrow><mi>z</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>z</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> be two complex numbers satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>|</mo><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>|</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn></math> and <math><mo>|</mo><msub><mrow><mi>z</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>i</mi><mo>|</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. Then the minimum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
question: Let <math><msub><mrow><mi>z</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>z</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> be two complex numbers satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>|</mo><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>|</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn></math> and <math><mo>|</mo><msub><mrow><mi>z</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>i</mi><mo>|</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. Then the minimum value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>z</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is :
solution:
We have,

C1:z1=9

C2:z2-3-4i=4

Centres of the circles are c1≡0,0 and c2≡3,4.

Radius of the circles are r1=9 and r2=4.

⇒c1c2=32+42=5 and r1-r2=5

Circles touch each other internally.

⇒z1-z2min=0 at the point of contact.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $S=\{z \in \mathbb{C}: z^2+4z+16=0\}$. Then $\sum_{z \in S}|z+\sqrt{3}i|^2$ is equal to:
question: Let $S=\{z \in \mathbb{C}: z^2+4z+16=0\}$. Then $\sum_{z \in S}|z+\sqrt{3}i|^2$ is equal to:
solution:
Given the equation $z^2 + 4z + 16 = 0$, we find its roots using the quadratic formula:

$z = \dfrac{-4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 64}}{2} = \dfrac{-4 \pm \sqrt{-48}}{2} = -2 \pm 2\sqrt{3}i$

The set $S$ contains two complex numbers: $z_1 = -2 + 2\sqrt{3}i$ and $z_2 = -2 - 2\sqrt{3}i$.

We need to evaluate the sum $\sum_{z \in S} |z + \sqrt{3}i|^2$, which is $|z_1 + \sqrt{3}i|^2 + |z_2 + \sqrt{3}i|^2$.

For $z_1 = -2 + 2\sqrt{3}i$:

$|z_1 + \sqrt{3}i|^2 = |-2 + 2\sqrt{3}i + \sqrt{3}i|^2 = |-2 + 3\sqrt{3}i|^2$

$|-2 + 3\sqrt{3}i|^2 = (-2)^2 + (3\sqrt{3})^2 = 4 + 27 = 31$

For $z_2 = -2 - 2\sqrt{3}i$:

$|z_2 + \sqrt{3}i|^2 = |-2 - 2\sqrt{3}i + \sqrt{3}i|^2 = |-2 - \sqrt{3}i|^2$

$|-2 - \sqrt{3}i|^2 = (-2)^2 + (-\sqrt{3})^2 = 4 + 3 = 7$

Adding these values together:

$\sum_{z \in S} |z + \sqrt{3}i|^2 = 31 + 7 = 38$

Answer: $38$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> respectively be the modulus and amplitude of the complex number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>i</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>tan</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>5</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>θ</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> respectively be the modulus and amplitude of the complex number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>z</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>i</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>tan</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>5</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>8</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>r</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>θ</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to
solution:
Given: z=2-i2tan5π8

⇒z=21-itan5π8

⇒z=21-isin5π8cos5π8

⇒z=2cos5π8cos5π8-isin5π8

⇒z=2cosπ-3π8cosπ-3π8-isinπ-3π8

⇒z=2-cos3π8-cos3π8-isin3π8

⇒z=2cos3π8cos3π8+isin3π8

⇒z=2sec3π8ei·3π8

Now on comparing with z=zeiθ we get,

⇒θ=3π8, r=2sec3π8

subject: Mathematics
q: The sum of all possible values of $\theta \in[-\pi, 2 \pi]$, for which $\frac{1+i \cos \theta}{1-2 i \cos \theta}$ is purely imaginary, is equal
question: The sum of all possible values of $\theta \in[-\pi, 2 \pi]$, for which $\frac{1+i \cos \theta}{1-2 i \cos \theta}$ is purely imaginary, is equal
solution: $\begin{aligned} & Z=\frac{1+i \cos \theta}{1-2 i \cos \theta} \\ & Z=-\bar{Z} \Rightarrow \frac{1+i \cos \theta}{1-2 i \cos \theta}=-\left(\frac{\overline{1+i \cos \theta}}{1-2 i \cos \theta}\right) \\ & (1+i \cos \theta)(\overline{1-2 i \cos \theta})=-(1-2 i \cos \theta)(\overline{1+i \cos \theta}) \\ & (1+i \cos \theta)(1+2 i \cos \theta)=-(1-2 i \cos \theta)(1-i \cos \theta) \\ & 1+3 i \cos \theta-2 \cos ^2 \theta=-\left(1-3 i \cos \theta-2 \cos ^2 \theta\right) \\ & 2-4 \cos ^2 \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \cos ^2 \theta=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=-\frac{\pi}{4},-\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{4}, \frac{5 \pi}{4}, \frac{7 \pi}{4} \\ & \text { sum }=3 \pi\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of complex numbers $z$ such that $|z-1|=|z+1|=|z-i|$ equals
question: The number of complex numbers $z$ such that $|z-1|=|z+1|=|z-i|$ equals
solution:
Let $z=x+i y$
$$
\begin{array}{lll}
|z-1|=|z+1| & \Rightarrow \operatorname{Re} z=0 & \Rightarrow x=0 \\
|z-1|=|z-i| & \Rightarrow x=y & \\
|z+1|=|z-i| & \Rightarrow y=-x &
\end{array}
$$
Only $(0,0)$ will satisfy all conditions.
$\Rightarrow$ Number of complex number $z=1$
